title: Kerala Cabbage Thoran

subs.name: green beans (cut fine), beetroot or raw papaya
subs.notes: the thoran technique works for almost any firm vegetable

subs.name: desiccated coconut moistened with 1 tbsp warm water
subs.notes: fresh coconut gives a far superior texture; use desiccated only as a last resort

subs.name: any neutral oil
subs.notes: coconut oil is critical for authentic flavour

steps.text: In a small bowl, mix the grated coconut with the green chillies, shallots, turmeric and a pinch of salt. Rub everything together with your fingertips for 30 seconds to bruise the chillies and shallots — this releases their flavour into the coconut.
Heat coconut oil in a kadai on medium-high flame. Add mustard seeds and wait for them to splutter and pop. Add dried red chillies and curry leaves immediately and let them crackle for 15 seconds.
Add the shredded cabbage all at once. Season with salt and toss well. Cover with a lid and cook for 3 minutes — the steam trapped inside will start to soften the cabbage.

Remove the lid and add the coconut mixture. Toss everything together on high flame and stir-fry for 3–4 minutes until the cabbage is just tender with a little bite and the coconut is cooked through but not browned. The thoran must be dry — if any water has pooled at the bottom, cook uncovered until it evaporates completely.
Taste for salt and remove from heat. Serve as part of a Kerala rice meal alongside sambar, rasam and fried pappadam. Thoran is also excellent with chapati.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cabbage → green beans (cut fine), beetroot or raw papaya (the thoran technique works for almost any firm vegetable); coconut → desiccated coconut moistened with 1 tbsp warm water (fresh coconut gives a far superior texture; use desiccated only as a last resort); coconut-oil → any neutral oil (coconut oil is critical for authentic flavour)
